1Audio Preprocessing & Canonicalization

1.1 The Canonicalization Problem

The watermark payload contains a SHA-256 hash of the audio signal. For this hash to serve as a reliable integrity check, the same audio content must always produce the same hash regardless of whether it arrives as a stereo WAV, a mono MP3, or a 48 kHz recording. We achieve this by mapping every input to a canonical form: mono, 44,100 Hz, peak-normalized, float32.

1.2 Mono Conversion

A multi-channel signal xraw ∈ ℜN×C with C channels is reduced to mono by averaging:

xmono[n] = (1/C) ∑c=0C−1 xraw[n, c]

This ensures the SHA-256 hash computed in Stage 2 is independent of whether the audio was stored in stereo or mono format.

1.3 Resampling

If the input sampling rate fs ≠ 44100 Hz, the signal is resampled using polyphase filtering to a target length:

Ntarget = ⌊Ninput ⋅ 44100 / fs⌋

Resampling ensures the FFT bin spacing in Stage 6 is always Δf = 44100 / 4096 ≈ 10.77 Hz, so the 1–8 kHz embedding band always maps to the same bin indices [93, 743], regardless of input sample rate.

1.4 Peak Normalization

The signal is scaled so its peak amplitude is exactly 1:

x[n] = xmono[n] / maxn|xmono[n]|,   x[n] ∈ [−1, 1]

Without this, two recordings of the same content at different volume levels serialize to different byte sequences and produce different SHA-256 hashes, breaking integrity verification.

1.5 Frame Segmentation

The canonical signal x ∈ ℜN is divided into non-overlapping frames of length L. The k-th frame is:

xk[n] = x[n + k⋅L],   n = 0, 1, …, L−1
LFrame length: 4,096 samples (real system), 32 samples (toy)
kFrame index, k = 0, 1, 2, …
xk[n]n-th sample of the k-th frame

Non-overlapping frames are used deliberately. Overlapping frames require a synthesis window during reconstruction and an analysis window during detection. Applying two windows sequentially convolves the embedded spectrum with the window transform, smearing the PN-modulated magnitudes and degrading bit extraction accuracy.

🧪 Toy Example — Canonical 64-sample signal
x_raw[n] = 0.7⋅sin(2π⋅4⋅n/64) + 0.3⋅sin(2π⋅12⋅n/64), n = 0..63
SR = 64 Hz, N = 64 → after peak normalization:

n01234567
x[n]0.00.77081.00.75220.56570.75221.00.7708

Frame 0 = x[0:32], Frame 1 = x[32:64] (L = 32 for toy)
Expected spectral peaks: bin 4 (4 Hz) and bin 12 (12 Hz)

2SHA-256 Cryptographic Hashing

2.1 Generating a Fingerprint of the Audio

Once the canonical audio array x ∈ [−1,1]N is produced in Stage 1, we need a compact fixed-size representation that uniquely identifies its exact byte contents. We define this fingerprint as:

H = SHA256( tobytes(x) ) ∈ {0,1}256

where tobytes(⋅) serializes the float32 array into its raw IEEE 754 byte sequence — each sample becomes 4 bytes, so our 64-sample toy produces 256 input bytes. The result H is a 32-byte digest placed at bytes [13:45] of the 109-byte payload.

The hash must satisfy two properties for our system. First, collision resistance: it must be computationally infeasible to find x' ≠ x such that SHA256(x') = H, otherwise an attacker could substitute a different audio that passes the integrity check. Second, the avalanche effect: for any perturbation x' = x + ε, the expected number of output bits that differ is:

Ε[ ∑j=1256 1[Hj ≠ H'j] ] = 128

meaning any modification to the audio, no matter how small, flips approximately half the hash bits unpredictably.

2.2 The Merkle-Damgard Construction

SHA-256 processes the serialized audio bytes as a sequence of 512-bit blocks M1, M2, …, Mn through a compression function f:

H0 = IV,    Hi = f(Hi−1, Mi),    H = Hn
IVFixed 256-bit initialization vector (fractional parts of square roots of first 8 primes)
fCompression function: 64 rounds of XOR, rotations, modular additions over eight 32-bit words
nNumber of 512-bit blocks: for toy, 256 bytes = 2 blocks, so n = 2

2.3 Role at Detection

At detection, the received audio y is re-canonicalized and re-hashed: H' = SHA256(tobytes(y)). If the audio was modified after embedding, H' ≠ H. This mismatch is caught automatically — the Ed25519 signature in Stage 3 is computed over the block containing H, so any change to the audio causes signature verification to fail without requiring an explicit hash comparison step.

🧪 Toy Example — SHA-256 on canonical x
H = SHA256(x.tobytes())
= 2921ed43cb1b8120fd868f47db7a3387
1c2435f1329858394ea45960067aae8d

Perturb x[0] += 0.0001 (smallest meaningful change):
H' = SHA256(x'.tobytes())
= 2698bfe7cee034a0331cebbedf704af6
4a91f83a4def47dc5603f1968993f648

Bits changed: 135/256 = 52.7% (expected ~50% by avalanche effect)
H is placed at payload bytes [13:45] and re-verified at Stage 7.

3Ed25519 Digital Signature

3.1 From Integrity to Ownership

The hash H from Stage 2 tells us whether the audio was modified — but it says nothing about who created the watermark. Anyone could compute SHA-256 on any audio and embed it. To prove ownership, the hash must be signed with a private key that only the content creator holds. The Ed25519 digital signature binds the watermark payload to the creator's cryptographic identity.

3.2 The Signable Block and Signature

The signature is computed over a structured block that includes MAGIC, VERSION, OWNER_ID, and the hash, scoping it to this specific protocol and owner:

B = MAGIC (4B) || VERSION (1B) || OWNER_ID (8B) || H (32B) = 45 bytes
S = Ed25519.sign(sk,  B)   ∈ {0,1}512
skEd25519 private key, held only by the content creator
BSignable block, 45 bytes
S64-byte signature, placed at payload bytes [45:109]

3.3 Why Ed25519

Ed25519 operates over the Twisted Edwards curve Curve25519. It produces a compact 64-byte signature (RSA at equivalent security requires 256–512 bytes), requires no random nonce during signing making it immune to nonce-reuse attacks that affect ECDSA, and runs in constant time preventing timing side-channel attacks. The 64-byte size is critical here — it must fit within the 109-byte payload budget alongside the 32-byte hash and 13-byte header.

3.4 Verification

At detection, after extracting H, S, and the header fields from the decoded payload, the verifier reconstructs B and checks:

Ed25519.verify(pk,  B,  S)   →   True / InvalidSignature

This passes if and only if S was produced by the private key corresponding to pk over exactly block B. If the audio was tampered with, H changes, B changes, and verification fails.

🧪 Toy Example — Signing the hash
MAGIC = b'AWMK' (0x41574D4B)
VERSION = 0x01
OWNER_ID = DE AD BE EF 01 02 03 04
B = MAGIC + VERSION + OWNER_ID + H → 45 bytes

S = Ed25519.sign(sk, B) → 0560f9551d2543cd... (first 8 bytes)
Ed25519.verify(pk, B, S) → True AUTHENTICATED

S placed at payload bytes [45:109].
If H changes (tampered audio): verify() raises InvalidSignature.

4Watermark Payload Construction

4.1 Assembling the Payload

The hash H and signature S from Stages 2 and 3 are combined with a protocol header into the 109-byte payload P:

P = MAGIC (4B) || VERSION (1B) || OWNER_ID (8B) || H (32B) || S (64B)
|P| = 4 + 1 + 8 + 32 + 64 = 109 bytes = 872 bits

The MAGIC field gives the detector a fast sanity check — if the extracted payload does not begin with 0x41574D4B, it is immediately rejected without attempting RS decoding or signature verification. The VERSION field allows the protocol to evolve while maintaining backward compatibility.

4.2 Field Layout

FieldBytesSizePurpose
MAGIC[0:4]4 B0x41574D4B — fast rejection of invalid extractions
VERSION[4]1 B0x01 — protocol version for forward compatibility
OWNER_ID[5:13]8 BContent creator identifier
AUDIO_HASH[13:45]32 BSHA-256 digest of canonical audio — integrity proof
SIGNATURE[45:109]64 BEd25519 signature over B — authenticity proof

4.3 Serialization to Bit Stream

The payload P is serialized to a binary sequence MSB-first per byte to produce the watermark bit stream b ∈ {0,1}872:

b[8i + j] = (P[i] >> (7 − j)) & 1,   i = 0,…,108,   j = 0,…,7

This 872-bit stream is passed to the Reed-Solomon encoder in Stage 5, which expands it to 1,272 bits before embedding into the audio.

🧪 Toy Example — Payload assembly
P[0:4] = 41 57 4D 4B ("AWMK")
P[4] = 01
P[5:13] = DE AD BE EF 01 02 03 04
P[13:45] = 2921ed43cb1b8120fd868f47db7a3387
1c2435f1329858394ea45960067aae8d
P[45:109]= (Ed25519 signature S, 64 bytes)

|P| = 109 bytes
Bitstream b[0:8] from P[0]=0x41 ("A"): 0 1 0 0 0 0 0 1
First 4 bits after RS encoding (to embed): [0, 1, 0, 0]

5Reed-Solomon Error Correction

5.1 Why Error Correction is Necessary

When the watermarked audio undergoes signal processing attacks — noise addition, resampling, compression — some of the embedded bits will be flipped during extraction. A single flipped bit anywhere in the 64-byte Ed25519 signature corrupts it entirely, causing verification to fail. Reed-Solomon coding addresses this by adding redundant parity bytes to the payload before embedding, allowing the decoder to locate and correct corrupted bytes at extraction time.

5.2 Reed-Solomon over GF(28)

Reed-Solomon codes operate over the Galois Field GF(28), a finite field of 256 elements where each element is one byte. Addition is XOR: a ⊕ b. Multiplication uses a lookup table derived from the primitive polynomial p(x) = x8 + x4 + x3 + x2 + 1.

Given a message of k data symbols, the encoder treats it as coefficients of a polynomial m(x) over GF(28) and appends n−k parity symbols computed as the remainder of m(x)⋅xn−k divided by the generator polynomial g(x):

c(x) = m(x)⋅xn−k − [ m(x)⋅xn−k mod g(x) ]

Any t corrupted symbols can be corrected provided:

2t ≤ n − k   ⇒   t ≤ ⌊(n−k)/2⌋

5.3 Parameters in Our System

We use RS(159, 109) with 50 parity symbols appended to the 109-byte payload:

n = 159,   k = 109,   n−k = 50   ⇒   t = ⌊50/2⌋ = 25 correctable byte errors

The 159-byte codeword serializes to 159 × 8 = 1,272 bits — the total number of watermark bits embedded in Stage 6.

🧪 Toy Example — RS encoding of the 109-byte payload
Input: P = 109 bytes
Encoded: C = RS(159,109).encode(P) → 159 bytes
Parity: C[109:159] = 50 symbols over GF(2⁸)

First 8 bytes of P before encoding:
Index01234567
Hex41574D4B01DEADBE

Inject 5 byte errors at positions [10, 25, 40, 70, 100]:
RS(159,109).decode(corrupted_C) → original P recovered ✓
5 errors corrected of 25 available capacity
Total bits to embed: 159 × 8 = 1,272 bits

6Spread-Spectrum FFT Embedding

6.1 The FFT of an Audio Frame

Each frame xk ∈ ℜL is transformed into the frequency domain:

Xk[m] = ∑n=0L−1 xk[n]⋅e−j2πmn/L,   m = 0,1,…,L/2

Each coefficient Xk[m] ∈ ℂ carries magnitude |Xk[m]| and phase ∠Xk[m]. Only the magnitudes are modified during embedding — preserving phase keeps the temporal structure of the signal intact and limits perceptual distortion.

6.2 PN Chip Sequence Generation

For each watermark bit at global index i, a chip sequence pni ∈ {−1,+1}C of length C is generated deterministically:

seedi = SHA256(OWNER_ID || i),    pni[c] ∈ {−1,+1}

The sequence is unique per (OWNER_ID, bit index) pair, reproducible at the detector from OWNER_ID alone, and statistically orthogonal across different owners and bit positions — providing security against watermark removal without knowledge of OWNER_ID.

6.3 Multiplicative Embedding

Each bit b ∈ {0,1} is mapped to bipolar b' = 2b−1 ∈ {−1,+1}. The magnitudes at the C bins assigned to bit i are scaled:

|X'k[m]| = |Xk[m]|⋅(1 + α⋅b'⋅pni[m − bin_low − i⋅C])
αEmbedding strength = 0.35
b'Bipolar bit: +1 for bit=1, −1 for bit=0
pniPN chip sequence for bit i, length C = 31 (real) / 1 (toy)
bin_lowFirst FFT bin of embedding band: 93 at 1 kHz (real), 2 (toy)

The watermarked frame is reconstructed by inverse FFT with modified magnitudes and original phase:

x'k[n] = IFFT( |X'k[m]|⋅ej∠Xk[m] )
🧪 Toy Example — Embedding 4 bits into x, bins 2–5, C=1, α=0.35
Bits: b = [0, 1, 0, 0] (first 4 bits of RS-encoded payload)
Chips: p = [-1.0, 1.0, -1.0, 1.0] (from SHA256(OWNER_ID || i))

Scale factor per bin (1 + 0.35 * b' * p):
Bit 0: b'=-1, p=-1.0 → 1.3500
Bit 1: b'=+1, p=+1.0 → 1.3500
Bit 2: b'=-1, p=-1.0 → 1.3500
Bit 3: b'=-1, p=+1.0 → 0.6500

Original |FFT| at bins [2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0]: [0.0, 0.0, 31.6784, 0.0]
Modified |FFT| at bins [2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0]: [0.0, 0.0, 42.7658, 0.0]

SNR of watermarked signal: 9.85 dB
x'[0:8] = [-0.0, 0.9034, 1.245, 1.0723, 0.9122, 1.0723, 1.245, 0.9034]

7Watermark Detection & Signature Verification

7.1 Log-Magnitude Correlation

The received audio y is framed and FFT-transformed: Y[m] = FFT(yk[n]). Taking the log of the multiplicative embedding model converts it to an additive one:

log|X'[m]| ≈ log|X[m]| + α⋅b'⋅pni[m]

The correlation for bit i is computed as a dot product with the PN sequence, with mean subtraction to remove host-signal bias:

corri = (1/C) ∑c=0C−1 ( log|Y[bin_low + i⋅C + c]| − μ ) ⋅ pni[c]
b̂i = 1  if  corri > 0,   else   b̂i = 0

7.2 Multi-Offset Synchronization

A time-shift of δ samples misaligns extraction frames with embedding frames, corrupting all bits. The detector searches K=8 equally-spaced offsets within one hop:

δs = s⋅L/K,   s = 0,1,…,K−1

RS decoding is attempted at each offset. The first offset that produces a valid RS decode and correct MAGIC/VERSION is accepted for signature verification.

7.3 Authentication Decision

After RS decoding recovers the 109-byte payload, the fields are parsed, B is reconstructed, and the signature is verified:

Ed25519.verify(pk,  B,  Sext)   →   AUTHENTIC  /  TAMPERED
🧪 Toy Example — Extracting bits from watermarked x'
For each bit i: corr = (log|Y[BL+i]| - mean(log|Y[BL:BH]|)) * pn[i]

Bit 0: corr = +6.69540 → b̂=1 (embedded: 0) ✗
Bit 1: corr = -6.69538 → b̂=0 (embedded: 1) ✗
Bit 2: corr = -20.08617 → b̂=0 (embedded: 0) ✓
Bit 3: corr = -6.69538 → b̂=0 (embedded: 0) ✓

Toy match: 2/4 (C=1 chip/bit; real system C=31 gives ~14.9 dB processing gain)
Ed25519 verification: PASS ✓
Result: AUTHENTIC owner identity and content hash confirmed.

★End-to-End Transformation Summary

The table below captures the state of the same 64-sample toy signal at every checkpoint in the pipeline.

StageOperationInputOutputSize
1Canonicalizationx_raw (float32)x, normalized64×4 = 256 B
2SHA-256x.tobytes() (256 B)H (32-byte digest)32 B
3Ed25519 SignB = MAGIC+VER+OID+H (45B)S (signature)64 B
4Payload AssemblyMAGIC+VER+OID+H+SP109 B / 872 bits
5RS(159,109) EncodeP (109 B)C (codeword)159 B / 1,272 bits
6FFT Embedx + bits(C)x' (watermarked)64×4 = 256 B
7Detect & Verifyx' + OWNER_ID + pkDetectionResultauthentic / tampered
MetricToy ValueReal System
SNR9.85 dB>35 dB
Max residual amplitude0.346482<0.01
Avalanche (SHA-256)135/256 bits (52.7%)~128/256 (50%)
Chips per bit131 (~14.9 dB gain)
Bit match (toy, no RS)2/4 = 50%>98% (with RS)
RS error capacity25 byte errors25 byte errors
Ed25519 verificationPASSPASS (clean audio)
